pattern: Because a bit set anywhere in the final OR costs strictly more than every lower bit combined, the answer can be built one bit at a time from the most significant down, at each step checking whether every row still has SOME value compatible with keeping that bit at 0 — greedily forcing a bit to 1 only when a row has no other option.
hints: Picking the smallest value in each row independently does NOT always minimize the OR — two rows might each have a slightly-larger value that happens to share the same bits, avoiding an extra bit that their individual minimums would have introduced.

Build the answer bit by bit, from the highest bit down to the lowest. Keep a running mask of the bits already decided. At each new bit, ask: if this bit stays 0, is there still at least one value in EVERY row whose bits above (and including) this position fit inside what's already been decided?

A value 'fits' a tentative mask (decided bits, this bit at 0, all lower bits treated as free/don't-care) exactly when value OR mask equals mask. If every row has such a value, leave the bit at 0 and move to the next lower bit; if any row has no compatible value, that bit must be forced to 1 in the answer before continuing.

constraints.note: grid.length * grid[i].length <= 100000; every row has at least one element

edge_inputs.description: single row, single column — the answer is just that one value
single row with several candidates — pick the smallest value
picking the row-wise minimum from each row (1 and 2) gives OR=3, but picking 2 from both rows gives the smaller OR=2 — proves naive per-row minimum is not optimal
both rows are forced to the same single value — OR is unavoidable regardless of any strategy
three rows where only a shared low-bit value across all rows avoids setting any higher bit
two rows each with only one (large, max-value) candidate — no choice possible
single row containing the max value alongside 1 — the minimum is 1

stress.description: random small-to-medium grids with random row widths and values, seeded, covering many overlapping-bit scenarios
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = rng.randint(1, 50)
    n = rng.randint(1, 50)
    grid = [[rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [grid]

complexity.time: O(B * total elements) where B is the bit width (~17)
